title: podman build fails to pick up secrets from file in Windows
body:
### Issue Description

I'm unable to pass secrets into container during `podman build` in Windows. Same Dockerfile and script works fine on a Mac.

Will appreciate feedback and workarounds, if there are any...

### Steps to reproduce the issue

## Steps to reproduce the issue:
Dockerfile
```
FROM alpine

RUN --mount=type=secret,id=my_secret \
    ls -l /run/secrets/my_secret \
    && cat /run/secrets/my_secret \
    && echo && echo "Done!"
```

Script to build the image:
```
echo "my little secret" > secret.txt
podman build . --no-cache -t local/podman-secrets:latest --secret id=my_secret,src=secret.txt
```


### Describe the results you received

Output from the command:
```
time="2024-08-30T11:35:23+02:00" level=error msg="1 error occurred:\n\t* archive/tar: write too long\n\n\n"
Error: Post "http://d/v5.2.0/libpod/build?compatvolumes=0&dockerfile=%5B%22Dockerfile%22%5D&forcerm=1&httpproxy=1&identitylabel=1&idmappingoptions=%7B%22HostUIDMapping%22%3Atrue%2C%22HostGIDMapping%22%3Atrue%2C%22UIDMap%22%3A%5B%5D%2C%22GIDMap%22%3A%5B%5D%2C%22AutoUserNs%22%3Afalse%2C%22AutoUserNsOpts%22%3A%7B%22Size%22%3A0%2C%22InitialSize%22%3A0%2C%22PasswdFile%22%3A%22%22%2C%22GroupFile%22%3A%22%22%2C%22AdditionalUIDMappings%22%3Anull%2C%22AdditionalGIDMappings%22%3Anull%7D%7D&isolation=0&jobs=1&layers=1&networkmode=0&nocache=1&nsoptions=%5B%7B%22Name%22%3A%22user%22%2C%22Host%22%3Atrue%2C%22Path%22%3A%22%22%7D%5D&omithistory=0&output=local%2Fpodman-secrets%3Alatest&outputformat=application%2Fvnd.oci.image.manifest.v1%2Bjson&pullpolicy=missing&rm=1&secrets=%5B%22id%3Dmy_secret%2Csrc%3Dpodman-build-secret3443959728%22%5D&shmsize=67108864&t=local%2Fpodman-secrets%3Alatest": 
io: read/write on closed pipe
```

### Describe the results you expected

Secrets are passed into container during build and available to mount.

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Windows 11, 22H2

### Additional information

Same dockerfile and command works fine on a Mac with same version of Podman.

comments.body:
I suppose it is not a solution, but maybe it helps with investigation. I got the same issue when starting dev container in VS Code. I have Windows machine and podman desktop. When I used 
```--secret=id=my_token,src=sec.txt" in build.options in .devcontainer.json```
 I was getting the same error as shown in this issue. When I used 
 ```--volume=ABSOLUTE_PATH_TO_DIR_WITH_MY_TOKEN_FILE:/run/secrets```
  it is working.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Update to avoid slipping into oblivion...

Same issue here on Podman for Windows. I could build once using a secret, but now it fails reproducibly with the reported message.

Tested to replace docker with podman and felt onto the same problem.
Ran into same issue as well, using --volume work-around offered by @KarolGongola
also same error when trying 2026 VS Insiders with support for podman
Same error here. Does anyone has a workaround?

Still broken for me:

`Error: creating build executor: could not parse secrets: faccessat /home/user/a_token: no such file or directory`

Give this running on Windows - surprised to see `/home/user/a_token` as the source for the secret.

~~Could this be another case of client state vs container host state confusion in `--remote` mode, similar to https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/19844? (That's another situation where secrets aren't being loaded correctly due to `podman` attempting to look them up on the container host rather than on the client where `podman` is running)~~

Edit: no, this is failing on the API call.

~~I investigated further, and, at least for me, this specific failure mode was a bad interaction with Git Bash for Windows: `podman` doesn't see the POSIX filesystem emulation layer, so any paths from the emulation layer need to be resolved to actual windows filesystem paths with `cygpath -w $posix_path`  before being handed to `podman`.~~

I also got the `tar: write too long` failure described in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17899 after that, but `podman` was at least finding the locals secret file, writing it out to a secrets tarball for upload, and attempting to make the API call to the remote machine.

Edit: I misread the originally filed issue, so after sorting out the path problem, my remaining symptoms are the same as those the OP describes. The path problem is instead the one @tlindeb described.

https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/24283 reported a similar error message in a situation where a `zstd` size header was being calculated incorrectly.

The local temporary copies of the secrets files are being created without error (I took a copy during a build to check, which suggests the problem is arising somewhere either in the code that assembles that content to be transmitted as a tar stream in the request body (starting from https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/c995882ecfe9ef45dcf63da17c8d84c3baa04858/pkg/bindings/images/build.go#L1049), or else something in the actual creation of the tarball (`prepareRequestBody` is passed in as a parameter to `build`, so I haven't worked out where that is coming from yet).

It's hard to see how a Windows specific problem could be arising in the generation of the tar contents, suggesting it may be an issue in the Windows request preparation code (which appears to only have mutiple elements in the tar content list if the container file is outside the context directory, or at least one secret is specified). (If that hypothesis is valid, Windows image build requests would also have problems when the container definition is outside the context directory)

I'm not set up to build or test Go projects, but following the remote request path down for `build(...)` ends up in the `ntar(...)` writing function: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/c995882ecfe9ef45dcf63da17c8d84c3baa04858/pkg/bindings/images/build.go#L1189

On code inspection, the only way I can see for the file write and tar header info to get out of sync for a regular file is if `dentry.Info()` returns a smaller number than `io.Copy(...)` copies. My conclusion from that is that code inspection isn't going to reveal the problem here, rather than that actually being the explanation.
